prefazione.en: “Judges and consigns according to how he coils”
prefazione.note: Prefazione, p.7: centered, italic display line (Dante quotation), set off on its own line with surrounding quotation marks; native-resolution crop shows clearly slanted letterforms.
Prefazione, p.8: right-aligned author signature set in small caps (initial full-height capitals C, remaining letters capital forms at x-height); not the chapter-opening incipit.

p1_capitolo_primo.it_anchor: un uomo noto al Popolo ed ai suoi tiranni
p1_capitolo_primo.en_anchor: a man known to the People and to its tyrants
p1_capitolo_primo.note: Crespi, p.11 (scan page_0011), left column, mid-paragraph 'un uomo noto al Popolo ed ai suoi tiranni'. 'Popolo' set in visibly heavier/darker strokes than surrounding roman text (concept-word emphasis, same convention as Mazzini's 'Popolo'). English 'People' is capitalized in en_text and carries the same emphasis intent.

p1_capitolo_quarto.it_anchor: amicissimo della mia famiglia: Pietro Fortunato Calvi
p1_capitolo_quarto.en_anchor: dear friend of my family: Pietro Fortunato Calvi
p1_capitolo_quarto.note: Dante n/a — Crespi, p.26 (left column, last line): in the patriot's first introduction "famiglia: Pietro Fortunato Calvi..." the words 'Fortunato Calvi' are set in clearly heavier (bold) strokes while 'Pietro' is roman; verified in native-resolution crop. The other three mentions of 'Pietro Fortunato Calvi' (p.26 right col, p.27 left col, p.28) are plain roman — only the first introduction is bold, hence the anchor.

p1_capitolo_decimo_quinto.note: Dante quotation set off as an indented, bold display line on p.77, right column (lone line with whitespace, heavier strokes than surrounding paragraph text). Categorized as verse for the set-off centered display rendering; EN carries it as an italicized inline quotation.

p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.it: "Mazzini è in sul loco ove si trema
p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.en: *"Mazzini is in the place where men tremble*
p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.note: Dante/dall'Ungaro verse quotation, p.85 right column, four-line centered set-off stanza in quotation marks; line 1

p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.en: *That the hour supreme may reach the traitor's ear;*
p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.note: verse stanza p.85 centered set-off line 2

p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.it: "Mazzini è in ogni loco ove si opera
p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.en: *Mazzini is in every place where men labor*
p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.note: verse stanza p.85 centered set-off line 3

p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.en: *To shed their blood for Italy entire!"*
p1_capitolo_decimo_settimo.note: verse stanza p.85 centered set-off line 4

p1_capitolo_ventesimoterzo.note: p.116, left column, mid-page: 'difendere pena la morte, aveva' — 'pena la morte' set in visibly heavier strokes than surrounding roman; 'pena la' ends one line, 'morte,' begins the next, both bold.
p.119, footnote (1), second line: 'Francesco Giuseppe si recò a Venezia' — name set in bold, clearly heavier than the surrounding footnote roman.

p2_capitolo_terzo.note: Dante p.140 (printed folio 134), right column: in the line 'altri uomini, altri amori!' the final word 'amori' is set in visibly heavier strokes than the surrounding roman; the other 'amori' (p.137 'grandi nei loro amori') is plain roman, hence the anchor.

p2_capitolo_sesto.en: "Under the hauberk of feeling himself pure,"
p2_capitolo_sesto.note: Crespi p.150 (scan page 150), right column: Dante quotation (Inferno XXVIII) set on its own centered line, offset from the surrounding prose. Set in roman type (not slanted) but typographically set off as a display verse line. Quotation marks included per convention.

p2_capitolo_dodicesimo.it_anchor: cambiando in t il suono del th e facendo
p2_capitolo_dodicesimo.note: p2_capitolo_dodicesimo, scan page_0170.png (folio 164), right column. Phonetic letter-pair set in bold roman (heavier strokes than surrounding text) in the passage on Orsini's pronunciation of 'thirty'. Confirmed in native-resolution crop /tmp/d3.png. English en_text already encodes this as markdown italic (*th*), so en left empty to avoid double-styling.

p2_capitolo_dodicesimo, scan page_0170.png (folio 164), right column. Standalone phonetic letter 't' set in bold roman ('cambiando in t il suono del th'). First 't' inside the anchor is the bold standalone letter. Confirmed in crop /tmp/d3.png. English en_text already carries *t* italic, so en left empty.
Crespi p.170: phonetic reference — only the letter 'r' (after the elided article l') is bold roman; l' is plain. Narrowed from 'l'r' per native-crop verification so the article is not emboldened.

p2_capitolo_sedicesimo.note: Crespi, p.187, right column: in 'percorresse frequentemente, a cavallo, le vie della metropoli' the phrase 'a cavallo' is set in visibly heavier (bold) type than the surrounding roman; concept-emphasis bold. English carries no styling in the source; mapped to the corresponding 'rode frequently'.

p2_capitolo_sedicesimo.it_anchor: di star di fronte, senza tremiti
p2_capitolo_sedicesimo.note: Crespi, p.189, left column: in 'incapaci di star di fronte, senza tremiti' the word 'star' is upright but visibly heavier (bold) than surrounding roman; emphasis bold. Italian fragment 'star' is unique in it_text. English 'standing' (within 'standing without trembling') is the corresponding word.

p2_capitolo_decimonono.en: why should I not say it
p2_capitolo_decimonono.it_anchor: Quanto a me perchè non dovrei dirlo! Quei momenti
p2_capitolo_decimonono.en_anchor: As for me... why should I not say it? Those moments
p2_capitolo_decimonono.note: Crespi, p.204 (scan page_0204), left column. "non dovrei dir-" / "-lo" is set in visibly heavier bold strokes versus the surrounding roman ("Quanto a me.... perchè"). Word hyphenated across the line break; the "lo" reads regular weight but the emphasis unit is the full phrase "non dovrei dirlo". English carries no original markup (translation); en fragment supplied so the concept-emphasis can map to both editions.

p2_capitolo_ventesimo_ottavo.it: precipitò tra le braccia priva di sensi
p2_capitolo_ventesimo_ottavo.note: Crespi, folio 238 (page_0244.png), right column. Full line set in visibly heavier strokes than the surrounding roman text: 'precipitò tra le braccia priva di sensi.' The preceding 'Mi' (end of prior line) is roman; the bold span begins at 'precipitò' and runs through 'sensi'. Verified on a native-resolution crop. English carries no corresponding bold (the translation 'She fell senseless into my arms.' is plain roman), so en is left empty.
